abstract: This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the spatial structure of Polish agriculture based on data from the 2010 Agricultural Census. By evaluating six distinct groups of internal agricultural factors—land quality and use, agrarian structure, socio-demographic characteristics, technical equipment, production structure, and economic performance—the study develops a composite index to measure the general level of agricultural development and structural health. These spatial patterns are then analyzed alongside key external determinants, including natural conditions, historical legacies, urbanization levels, and the distribution of financial support through the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP). The findings reveal that the diverse spatial landscape of Polish agriculture is only minimally influenced by natural environmental factors. Instead, among human-driven determinants, historical regional boundaries remain the most significant factor shaping the industry today, persisting even despite the high absorption of European Union funds.
